upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows an extraordinarily stable atmosphere: the surface-to-850mb lapse rate is only 0.13°C/km against a dry adiabatic rate of 9.8°C/km, indicating a strong subsidence inversion capping thermals well below 850mb (~1500m MSL). At 850mb, temps are 15.4°C with a massive 23°C spread, confirming extremely dry air aloft — thermals, if any, will be weak and capped low. Winds veer from S at surface to WNW 9 kts at 850mb and WSW 28-29 kts at 500mb, suggesting moderate wind shear in the mid-levels but light-and-variable conditions in the thermal layer.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for Bay Area. KSFO reporting NW 12 kts, high broken-overcast cloud deck (BKN200-OVC250), temps 21°C — classic post-frontal afternoon clearing. KRHV calm winds, BKN080 ceiling at 2800 ft AGL, suggesting mid-level cloud suppressing thermals at Ed Levin. KHAF clear with light S winds 3-7 kts and SST 12.8°C — no active marine layer at coast but cool SST. KAPC 20 kts southerly 10 kts, CLR, 22°C — inland valley warming nicely. KWVI CLR, SW 7-9 kts, 17-19°C.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin high launch is a thermal-primary site that also benefits from the valley sea breeze. Ideal conditions are light-to-moderate SW/W winds 8-15 kts at ridge height with a solid thermal trigger. The sea breeze typically arrives from the bay by midday, cycling from light thermals in the morning to ridge-assisted soaring in the afternoon.
sites.todaySetup: The dominant story today is the strong low-level inversion confirmed by the KOAK sounding — lapse rate of 0.13°C/km is essentially isothermal from surface to 850mb, which will severely suppress thermal development. Boundary layer heights stay under 500m through the afternoon (GFS/HRRR agree: BL tops at 725-800m at best around 2-3 PM). KRHV is reporting calm winds and a broken ceiling at 2800 ft AGL all afternoon, reinforcing the inversion story. Winds at the launch are very light all morning (under 4 kts, variable direction) and gradually back to the WNW at 7-8 kts by late afternoon — plausible ridge lift window but with 100% cloud cover all day from GFS/HRRR and that ceiling suppressing thermals, the air will feel dead and punchy. The slight positive is the afternoon NW/W component (8 kts at 4 PM per HRRR) could provide some ridge lift on the SW-facing launch, but the thermal engine is essentially off under this inversion.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 1-3 kts, highly variable (S-SW-W), overcast. BL only 35-370m. No thermal or ridge lift possible. Do not fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds picking up slightly to 5 kts SW, BL reaches 465-800m by 2 PM — marginal thermal height but inversion lid still firm. Overcast 100%. Conditions technically on-site but quality is poor with weak, unpredictable lift.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window of the day with 7-8 kts W/NW by 3-4 PM providing some ridge lift component. BL 505-725m. Still fully overcast, thermals suppressed, but light ridge soaring may be possible for experienced pilots comfortable in stable air.

verdict.hazards: Strong inversion caps thermals, 100% overcast all day, variable wind direction in morning, KRHV ceiling BKN080 suppressing upper air, potential for rotor in lee of ridge with variable flow

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft training hill at Ed Levin is a student/beginner slope requiring consistent light SW winds of 6-12 kts for safe ridge soaring or foot-launch practice. It's protected from strong gradient winds but needs enough flow to maintain altitude above the slope.
sites.todaySetup: The 600 ft launch faces the same inversion problem as the high launch — a crushed thermal environment all day with the BL rarely exceeding 400-500m. Winds at this lower level are even lighter than the ridge through midday (under 4 kts, variable S-SW-W), making even simple ridge soaring impossible. The afternoon sees 8 kts WNW at the surface by 4-6 PM (HRRR: 309° at 7-8 kts), which actually overshoots the ideal window for this training hill — the direction is too cross/north for the SW-facing slope. KRHV calm and stable all day confirms the valley floor isn't heating.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds effectively calm, 1-3 kts highly variable. No usable lift. Not suitable for any flight.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light S-SW winds 4-6 kts developing — approaching the lower threshold but direction is marginal (too southerly) for the SW-facing hill. Borderline for experienced students only.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind clocks to W-NW 8 kts by 4-5 PM — now too cross for the SW slope. The direction window is missed by the time speed is adequate. Not ideal for training operations.

verdict.wind: 1-4 kts variable AM, 8 kts WNW PM (wrong direction)
verdict.hazards: Winds too light and variable all morning, wrong direction (W-NW) when speed finally arrives in afternoon, strong inversion eliminates thermal assistance

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-to-NW-facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site driven by the Pacific sea breeze and onshore gradient. It requires W-SW wind at 10-20 kts to produce reliable ridge lift along the bluff. Too light and you sink out; too strong (25+ kts) and it's dangerous. Marine layer clears when SST is warm enough to allow mixing.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is the standout site today. The afternoon sea breeze delivers strong, usable WSW ridge lift. HRRR and GFS agree: winds build from 7 kts SW at 10 AM to 12-13 kts WSW by 2-3 PM, from an ideal 244-253° direction. KHAF is reporting clear skies with light southerly flow and SST of 12.8°C — the relatively cool SST and 10-second wave period suggest a modest marine influence, but the current METAR shows clear conditions and 16°C temps, meaning no active marine layer suppression. KSFO is already reporting 12 kts NW at 2100Z, confirming the afternoon gradient is solidly on. The buoy at SF Bar (46026) shows only 1.9 kts out of 230° — the offshore zone is light, meaning the sea breeze is driven by thermal gradient inland rather than a strong offshore fetch, which typically produces cleaner, less gusty coastal flow. Winds of 10-13 kts WSW through the 2-5 PM window are well within the ideal sweet spot for this site.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds 2-7 kts SW, variable and light — below ridge-soaring threshold. The bluff is flyable by expert P3s checking conditions, but sustained lift is unreliable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds building to 7-12 kts WSW by noon-2 PM. Ridge lift becoming consistent and workable. Good entry window as sea breeze establishes.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Prime window: 12-13 kts WSW, steady from 244-253°. Clean ridge lift along the bluff. Manageable for P2+ in this range; monitor for any gusty pulses noted by Tomorrow.io tomorrow.

verdict.hazards: Morning flow too light and variable; cool SST (12.8°C) could delay full sea breeze establishment; watch for late-day wave period swell interactions increasing turbulence near beach LZ

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak relies on a combination of valley thermals and the afternoon bay sea breeze funneling through the Fremont plain. SW winds of 8-15 kts with a healthy BL height make for classic ridge-thermal soaring. The site is sensitive to the inversion — if the lid is too low, thermals are punchy and short.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same inversion ceiling problem as Ed Levin — the KOAK sounding's near-zero lapse rate severely limits thermal development, with BL tops modeled at 370-800m through the afternoon. HRRR and GFS agree on the wind picture: under 4 kts variable in the morning, building to 5-8 kts SW by afternoon. KRHV calm and capped under BKN080 all day — this station closest to the inland thermal source confirms the valley is not heating aggressively. The wind speeds in the afternoon (7-8 kts W/NW by 4 PM) are below the site's reliable soaring threshold, and the thermal engine is suppressed by the inversion. Unlike Mussel Rock which gets clean sea breeze ridge lift, Mission Peak needs thermals to work well, and thermals simply aren't happening today under this cap.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Calm to 3 kts, variable. No thermal or ridge lift. Not flyable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light SW winds 3-5 kts developing. BL reaches 400-800m but capped by inversion. Weak, unreliable thermals at best. Not worth the hike.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: 7-8 kts W/NW by 3-4 PM provides a marginal ridge component but direction is slightly cross. Under the inversion cap and 100% cloud cover, thermal quality remains poor. Experienced P3+ only for a punchy, low-ceiling session.

verdict.hazards: Strong subsidence inversion caps thermals below 800m, 100% overcast suppressing solar heating, KRHV ceiling BKN080 confirms, winds light and variable in morning with potential rotor risk in afternoon cross-flow

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tomorrow (Tuesday Mar 31) looks gusty and overcast. HRRR shows 10-11 kts S-SSW at 8-10 AM building from an unusual southerly direction — likely a pre-frontal or post-trough gradient. Tomorrow.io flags gusts to 14.4 kts by afternoon. BL tops only 200-570m all day. 100% cloud cover throughout. The southerly surface wind is cross or even tailwind for SW-facing launch. MARGINAL at best for experienced pilots mid-afternoon if direction improves to WSW; otherwise NO FLY for most.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday at 600 ft launch: southerly 7-8 kts in morning backing to WSW by afternoon. BL stays low (235-645m). Tomorrow.io shows gusts 11-14 kts by 2-4 PM. Still 100% overcast. Wrong direction in morning, gusty and cloudy all day. NO FLY.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday looks robust but windy. HRRR: 9-12 kts SW at 8 AM building to 7-11.5 kts SW through midday. Tomorrow.io is more bullish with 12-14 kts SSW all day and gusts to 18 kts by 4 PM — that's approaching the upper limit for comfortable flying. The SSW direction at 209-238° is on the strong side of ideal. Flyable for P3+ in the morning window before gusts peak; P2s should sit out the gusty afternoon. MARGINAL-to-FLY in the AM, watch gusts carefully.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Tuesday: HRRR shows 10-11 kts SSW in the morning, easing to 5-9 kts by afternoon. Tomorrow.io flags gusts 9-14 kts. BL only 205-570m, 100% overcast all day. The SSW direction is generally flyable for Mission Peak but the low ceiling and strong inversion kill thermal potential. If the cloud breaks and direction clocks to SW, marginal afternoon window for P3+. Otherwise MARGINAL/NO FLY.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday Apr 1: GFS shows significant change — BL heights jump to 1685-1885m by afternoon, suggesting the inversion breaks. But precip probability hits 40-43% with high CAPE (up to 40 J/kg). Winds 10-13 kts SSW with southerly component. This looks like a potentially unstable day with convective risk — strong thermals possible but cumulus/CB development likely. Not suitable without careful morning re-assessment.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Wednesday Apr 1: Same story — deep BL (1685-1885m), 39-43% precip chance, gusty 10-13 kts southerly. If it's a clean convective day it could be spectacular, but precip probability and southerly gradient suggest a weather system. Assess carefully day-of.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Wednesday Apr 1: GFS shows 10-14 kts WSW, BL 540-940m, but 34-52% precip probability and 50 J/kg CAPE by afternoon — possible showers/instability. Mussel Rock could see strong sea breeze combined with convective activity, making for a complex and potentially hazardous day. Needs careful morning reassessment.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Wednesday Apr 1: Deep BL (1635-1735m), 10-15 kts SSW, 40-41% precip chance. Thermal potential is much higher than today but the precip and southerly gradient raise flags for weather system influence. Strong conditions for P3+ if it stays dry; convective overdevelopment risk in the afternoon.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: No GFS data beyond Apr 1 in this dataset. Based on the trajectory of increasing instability and precip probability through Wednesday, Thursday may bring continued unsettled conditions or a clearing pattern depending on storm track. Monitor evening updates.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Same as high launch — insufficient data for Day 3 beyond the trend toward unsettled weather mid-week. Check updated models Thursday morning.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Day 3 data unavailable in this dataset. The coastal site may recover faster post-frontal than inland sites if any system moves through Wednesday. Monitor buoy SST and KHAF METARs Thursday morning.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Day 3 beyond available data range. If Wednesday's potential system clears, Thursday could reset to a post-frontal pattern similar to today — stable but clearing. Watch for the pattern.

watchlist: Strong subsidence inversion (0.13°C/km lapse rate) suppresses thermals at all inland sites today — do not expect thermal flight at Ed Levin or Mission Peak
KRHV BKN080 ceiling all afternoon confirms low thermal ceiling (~2800 ft AGL) for inland sites
Mussel Rock afternoon sea breeze 12-13 kts WSW is the only reliable flying today — monitor for gusty pulses as gradient strengthens
Tuesday gusts at Mussel Rock forecast 15-18 kts (Tomorrow.io) — upper limit for comfortable P2 flying, P3+ only in afternoon
Wednesday Apr 1 shows sharp pattern change: deep BL, 40%+ precip, CAPE >40 J/kg — potential convective day or approaching system, reassess that morning
Cool SST at Half Moon Bay buoy (12.8°C) with 10s wave period — marine layer can redevelop quickly if onshore flow increases; watch KHAF METARs
tldr: Today is a one-site day — Mussel Rock wins with 11-13 kts clean WSW sea breeze arriving by 1 PM, while every other site is crushed under a brutal subsidence inversion (basically no thermals, low ceilings). If you're not driving to Daly City, tomorrow and Wednesday may be worth watching for improvement inland, though Tuesday is gusty and Wednesday has convective question marks.
